summary: DHH reports that Apple rejected the HEY Calendar companion app from the App Store, claiming it 'doesn't do anything' because it requires an existing account login. He argues this is hypocritical since major apps like Salesforce, Netflix, JPMorgan, and Google Calendar all require existing accounts. He points out that HEY already fought and won a similar battle with Apple in 2020, securing a specific carve-out for email services, and that HEY Calendar is a free companion to that same service. DHH frames this as evidence of Apple's unchecked monopoly power and expresses hope that the EU's Digital Markets Act and an imminent DOJ antitrust lawsuit will force change.
key_points: Apple rejected HEY Calendar after a 19-day review, claiming it 'doesn't do anything' because it requires an existing account — a standard practice for thousands of apps
HEY already fought and won an App Store battle in 2020, securing a specific carve-out for email services under rule 3.1.3(f)
Apple itself uses the same one-subscription-many-apps strategy with iCloud (Mail, Calendar, Files), making the rejection hypocritical
Apple selectively enforces rules, granting exceptions to mega corporations while inventing new rules for smaller companies
The EU Digital Markets Act and an imminent DOJ antitrust lawsuit represent potential regulatory relief
money_quotes: There is no rule of law with the app stores, except that of the jungle, and Apple is the 800 lbs gorilla, ruling as it sees fit.
After spending 19 days to review our submission, causing us to miss a long-planned January 2nd launch date, Apple rejected our stand-alone free companion app 'because it doesn't do anything'.
Apple has become so emboldened by a decade of free monopoly reign that they don't even feign a superficial adherence to their own rules.
They carve out exceptions left and right to mega corporations they'd rather not anger, then invent entirely new rules that aren't codified anywhere when it suits them.
We're never going to roll over and pay Apple 30% in protection money to be left alone.
The last time the DOJ sued Microsoft in the late 90s/early 2000s, it inflicted a serious wound on Redmond's capacity to capture markets, which gave us the rise of Google, Apple, and others.
You don't get something for nothing.
key_insight: Apple's App Store review process operates as an arbitrary monopoly power, selectively enforcing rules against smaller companies while exempting mega corporations, and only regulatory intervention can restore fair competition.
